missing_perspectives.who: Ibu Soko and female customary leaders (Adat Perpatih)
missing_perspectives.what: v2 mentions 'Ibu Soko Klana Hulu' but does not examine their role. Female customary leaders whose consent is required. Under Adat Perpatih, Ibu Soko are respected eldest women who control ancestral land (harta pusaka) and clan continuity, with substantial authority even though formal political offices are held by men.
missing_perspectives.why: The customary rejection of Mubarak's declaration came not only from male officeholders but from female customary leadership whose consent is essential under Adat Perpatih. Matrilineal inheritance: women control ancestral land and clan continuity.

missing_perspectives.who: B40 communities across all ethnic groups in NS
Rural communities in luak territories (Sungai Ujong, Jelebu, Johol, Rembau) where Adat Perpatih is lived practice

alternative_framings.current: Legal authority question: whether dismissed Undang can participate + what three eligible Undangs may do.
alternative_framings.alternative: Crisis of Adat Perpatih's democratic and consensual character. Perbilangan adat: 'muafakat lalu dalam golap, adat lalu di tongah torang' (consensus reached in darkness, custom proclaimed in light). Mubarak's unilateral declaration — without consent of Telaga Undang, Ibu Soko, or full consensus of all Undangs — violates core procedural norm regardless of formal legal standing.

1967 bypass of Tuanku Muhriz is constitutional precedent establishing Undang's electoral discretion as substantive, not ceremonial. 2008 election was 'revisitation' of that 1967 decision. Current Undangs naming Tunku Nadzaruddin — son of former Ruler Ja'afar — as successor continues the 1967-2008 succession question.
alternative_framings.primary_source: The Edge + Malay Mail explainer 20 Apr 2026

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Constitution of Lesotho (1993) Chapter V Article 45: College of Chiefs may designate successor per customary law. Theoretically empowered under traditional law to elect and depose King by majority vote.
Constitution 2008 Article 2 Section 21: Druk Gyalpo shall abdicate for willful constitutional violations or permanent mental disability, on motion passed by joint Parliament.

counter_arguments.counter: If Undang Yang Empat collectively possess power to elect YDPB, arguable they retain residual power to remove in extraordinary circumstances. Perbilangan adat: 'if a leader is elevated through muafakat, then through muafakat also their position can be questioned and brought down.'

Mubarak's 2025 dismissal itself may be contested on procedural grounds. Exact nature of 33 offences not publicly disclosed. 2016 Johol precedent: Mohd Jan Abdul Ghani's representatives claimed dismissal was 'premature' and not decided in DKU meeting; sought Special Court for Rulers referral.

missing_facts.fact: Sarifah Norzaidah Al-Qadri (Telaga Undang): Ibu Soko Klana Hulu and Anak Waris Klana Hulu never gave Mubarak a mandate; matter referred to NS Islamic Religious Affairs Department + police.

Mubarak's dismissal announced by Tua Lembaga Waris di-Darat Datuk Andulika Mandalika Zainol Ariffin Ibrahim after consent from Ibu Soko Klana Hulu + Anak Waris Klana Hulu, following Telaga Undang Sungai Ujong meeting 1 April 2025.
14th Undang Luak Johol Datuk Mohammad Jan Abdul Ghani dismissed 27 Mar 2016 for 13 offences — precedent for Clause 14(3).
Tuanku Muhriz invoked Clause 24 during 17 April 2026 DKU sitting to invite Ibu Soko + Waris Klana Hulu.
1967: Tuanku Muhriz (19) bypassed; Tuanku Ja'afar elected; reigned until 2008.
NS achieved RM19.1 billion approved investments in 2025 (+164% vs RM7.2B in 2024). MB targeted RM8 billion inflow for 2026.
Undang Yang Empat made declaration under perkara 10(1)(b) UUTKNS 1959. Provision allows Undangs to call upon Ruler to withdraw from performing duties.

gender_gaps.missing: Role of Ibu Soko whose consent is required for customary decisions including Mubarak's dismissal. Matrilineal system: women control ancestral land + clan continuity.
How dispute might differently affect women — rural women dependent on adat land; women in political leadership. Customary Tenure Enactment Chapter 215 codifies matrilineal inheritance.

economic_gaps.missing: No examination of economic implications — RM19.1B investments 2025 (+164%), RM8B target 2026. Constitutional uncertainty can affect investor perceptions.
Economic interests tied to customary land (tanah adat). Customary land is major economic asset for rural Malay communities in NS.

geographic_gaps.missing: Centered on Seremban, Seri Menanti, administrative/royal centres. No examination of perspectives in rural luak territories where Adat Perpatih is lived practice.
East Malaysia (Sabah/Sarawak) native customary law systems offer comparative insights.

overall_assessment: v2 substantially stronger than v1 in legal/constitutional framing. Still narrowly focused on institutional actors at apex. Omits: women customary leaders (Ibu Soko despite documented role), B40, youth, Orang Asli, grassroots adat in rural luak. Lacks economic analysis of dispute's impact on investment (RM19.1B 2025, RM8B target 2026). Would benefit from: 1967 context fuller, dismissal procedural details (Ibu Soko role), Lesotho/Bhutan comparatives. Completeness 78 reflects solid legal/governance but gaps in social/economic/gender/grassroots.
